g.data-narration: Welcome to CPU Scheduling Algorithms - the heart of how operating systems manage processor time efficiently.
CPU scheduling determines which ready process gets the CPU when multiple processes compete for processor time.
g.text: OS mechanism that selects which ready process gets CPU time. When multiple processes
compete, the scheduler determines execution order based on specific algorithms.

g.data-narration: There are two main types: preemptive allows interruption, while non-preemptive runs processes to completion.
We evaluate schedulers using metrics like waiting time, turnaround time, response time, and throughput.
First-Come-First-Served is the simplest algorithm using a FIFO queue - processes execute in order of arrival.
g.text: • Non-preemptive • Simple FIFO queue • No priority considerations
Convoy effect - long processes delay short ones

g.data-narration: Let's calculate with P1 at time 0, P2 at time 1, and P3 at time 2 - see how waiting times accumulate.
Shortest Job First selects the process with smallest burst time. It can be non-preemptive or preemptive (SRTF).
g.text: • Selects process with shortest burst time • Can be Non-preemptive or Preemptive (SRTF)
Requires knowing burst times in advance

g.data-narration: At time 0, only P1 exists. At time 2, P2 arrives with burst 3, and at time 4, P3 arrives with burst 4.
Priority scheduling runs highest priority first. It can cause starvation - solution is aging where priorities increase over time.
g.text: • Each process has a priority number • Highest priority runs first • Can be preemptive or non-preemptive
Starvation - low priority processes may wait indefinitely
Aging - gradually increase priority of waiting processes

g.data-narration: With priorities P1=3, P2=1, P3=2 (lower number = higher priority), execution order becomes P2, P3, P1.
g.text: Priority Scheduling Example (Lower number = Higher priority)

g.data-narration: Round Robin uses time quantum q. Each process gets q time units, then moves to queue end. Fair but high context switches.
g.text: • Preemptive • Uses time quantum (q) • Fair allocation - each process gets equal CPU time
1. Allocate CPU for q time units 2. After q expires, preempt and add to queue end 3. Repeat
Quantum should be 80% of average CPU burst

g.data-narration: With quantum=4, P1 runs 0-4, P2 runs 4-7, P3 runs 7-8, then P1 resumes 8-10. See the waiting times.
Compare algorithms: FCFS has high wait but no starvation, SJF has lowest wait but possible starvation, RR fair but high context switches.
Real systems use hybrid approaches: Linux CFS for fairness, Windows priority-based, and cloud schedulers with dynamic priorities.
Remember these essential formulas: TAT equals completion minus arrival, waiting time is TAT minus burst time.
For exams: always draw Gantt charts, watch for negative waiting times, remember quantum affects RR behavior, and SJF gives minimum average wait.
g.text: • Draw Gantt charts even if not asked - helps visualize
• Waiting time = Start - Arrival (not queue time)
• Large RR quantum → behaves like FCFS
• SJF gives minimum average waiting time (optimal!)
• Watch for negative waiting times (clamp to 0)
SJF is optimal for min avg WT!

g.data-narration: In summary: FCFS simple but causes convoy effect, SJF optimal but needs burst prediction, Priority risks starvation, and Round Robin is fair for time-sharing.
g.text: Master these algorithms to ace your OS exams! Practice numerical problems regularly.
